%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# 1.3 Periodic and Aperiodic Signals

> **Section Objective:** Learn how to recognize periodic signals, identify the fundamental period, and classify signals by their time support.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- Periodic signal
- Aperiodic signal
- Fundamental period
- Periodic extension
- One-period area property
- Everlasting signal
- Causal signal
- Noncausal signal
- Anti-causal signal

## 1. Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals

Eq. (1.7) is the exact periodicity test. Here \(x(t)\) is the signal, \(T_0 > 0\) is the candidate period, and **for all \(t\)** means the equality must hold at every single moment — not just at a few matching points.

Shifting the signal left by \(T_0\) must reproduce the original signal everywhere. If no positive \(T_0\) satisfies this for all time, the signal is **aperiodic**.

**Minimal example:** A sinusoid \(\cos(2\pi t)\) repeats after every 1 second — periodic. A single rectangular pulse fires once and is zero everywhere else — aperiodic.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Look for the phrase "repeats forever" or "find the period." If a problem asks you to find \(T_0\), you are applying Eq. (1.7).

### COMMON MISUSE

Do not check only one visible cycle or one matching point. The equality must hold for **all** \(t\), including times far outside the visible graph window.

$$x(t) = x(t + T_0) \quad \text{for all } t$$

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="book_image" data-teaching-role="example_support" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
![Figure 1.13](/figures/page-081-figure_1_13-1.png)
*Figure 1.13 shows how a full periodic signal is built by repeating any one-period segment end to end.*
<div class="lesson-figure-description">The figure contains two time-domain plots (a) and (b), each with horizontal axis \(t\) and vertical axis \(x(t)\). Each plot shows a piecewise waveform — a flat top followed by a downward ramp — with one highlighted (shaded) segment of duration \(T_0\). Identical copies of that segment are placed at equal time intervals to the left and right, extending the signal forever in both directions. Students should notice that the repeat spacing equals \(T_0\) and that the shaded segment can start anywhere, not necessarily at \(t = 0\).</div>

## 2. Fundamental period and periodic extension

For any periodic signal with period \(T_0\), the area under the curve over **any** interval of length exactly \(T_0\) is the same, regardless of where the interval starts. Here \(a\) and \(b\) are arbitrary starting points, \(T_0\) is the period, and \(x(t)\) is the periodic signal.

**When to use this:** Exam problems that ask for an integral over one full period, or that compare two one-period windows starting at different times.

**Minimal example:** Integrating from \(0\) to \(T_0\) gives the same result as integrating from \(3\) to \(3 + T_0\). Both windows have length \(T_0\), so the areas are equal.

### COMMON MISUSE

This property applies **only** when both integration windows have length exactly \(T_0\). An interval of length \(2T_0\) or \(T_0/2\) does not satisfy the equality in general.

$$\int_a^{a+T_0} x(t)\,dt = \int_b^{b+T_0} x(t)\,dt$$

## 3. Area over one period

This formula defines a **causal signal**: the signal is identically zero for all time before \(t = 0\). Here \(x(t)\) is the signal and the condition \(t < 0\) covers every moment before the origin.

**Minimal example:** A signal that switches on at \(t = 0\) and continues afterward is causal — nothing happens before the switch.

**Contrast:**
- A **noncausal** signal has at least one nonzero value before \(t = 0\).
- An **everlasting** signal exists for all \(t \in (-\infty, \infty)\) and is therefore always noncausal.
- **Periodic signals are everlasting by definition**, since the pattern must extend forever in both directions.

### EXAM TRIGGER

Inspect where the signal is nonzero relative to \(t = 0\). If any nonzero part lies before \(t = 0\), the signal is noncausal.

### COMMON MISUSE

Do not assume every noncausal signal is everlasting. A short pulse that occurs only on \(-2 < t < -1\) is noncausal but not everlasting.

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t < 0$$

## 4. Causal, noncausal, everlasting, and anti-causal signals

An **anti-causal signal** has no nonzero value at or after \(t = 0\). Every nonzero part must lie strictly before the origin.

**Minimal example:** A pulse defined only on \(-3 < t < -1\) is anti-causal — it is entirely finished before \(t = 0\).

### COMMON TRAP

Anti-causal is **stricter** than noncausal. A noncausal signal merely has *some* energy before \(t = 0\); an anti-causal signal has *no* energy at or after \(t = 0\). Every anti-causal signal is noncausal, but not every noncausal signal is anti-causal.

$$x(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } t \ge 0$$

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- A signal is periodic if \(x(t) = x(t + T_0)\) for all \(t\); otherwise it is aperiodic.
- The fundamental period is the smallest positive \(T_0\) satisfying the periodicity condition.
- For any periodic signal: \(\int_a^{a+T_0} x(t)\,dt = \int_b^{b+T_0} x(t)\,dt\) for all \(a, b\).
- Causal: \(x(t) = 0\) for \(t < 0\). Anti-causal: \(x(t) = 0\) for \(t \ge 0\). Everlasting: exists for all \(t\).
- Periodic signals are everlasting; noncausal does not imply everlasting.

*Next, we will use these classifications to analyze energy and power behavior of signals.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
